coredata.dc:title: Polyethylenimine-functionalized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots as effective fluorescent probes for detection of Hg2+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Kunfeng
Xia, Chongshu
Guo, Yahui
Yu, Hang
Xie, Yunfei
Yao, Weirong
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CDs) were performed using cysteine and polyethylenimine as raw materials. The prepared N, S-CDs exhibited excitation-independent in the range of 300–380 nm. Furthermore, mercury(II) ions (Hg2+) can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs. Based on this, we developed a fluorescence sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity to detect Hg2+. Under optimized conditions, the sensor showed good linearity in the range of 0–500 nM, and the limit of detection is 9.2 nM. Further, the sensor showed high sensitivity to Hg2+ in lake water and rice samples. The recovery of the Hg2+ in lake water and rice samples ranged between 98.2 % and 109.5 % with a relative standard deviation below 5.8 %. With outstanding sensitivity and selectivity, the fluorescence sensor provides a promising platform for monitoring Hg2+ in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped
Hg2+ detection
Fluorescence sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The N, S-CDs fluorescent sensors application in real samples
Sensitivity of the fluorescence probe for Hg2+
Selectivity of the fluorescent probe for Hg2+
Assay of N, S-CDs-based Hg2+ sensor in lake water and rice samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding This study was funded by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2020YFC1606800), the National first-class discipline program of Food Science and Technology (JUFSTR20180509), Science and technology project of Jiangsu Bureau of Quality and Technical Supervision (KJ175923 and KJ185646) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (2018 M642165 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HR-TEM of the N, S-CDs. The inset of (a) showed the size distribution of N, S-CDs. (c) XRD of N, S-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CDs.
(a) Zeta potential of the N, S-CDs. (b) Full-scan XPS spectrum, (c) high-resolution C 1 s, (d) high-resolution N 1 s, (e) high-resolution O 1 s and (f) high-resolution S 2p spectrum of the N, S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of N, S-CDs aqueous solutions. Insets in (a) show optical photographs of N, S-CDs aqueous solution under visible (left) and UV (right) light (365 nm). (b) PL spectra of N, S-CDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Effect of NaCl concentration on PL intensity of N, S-CDs. (b) Effect of pH on PL of N, S-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence of N, S-CDs quenched by Hg2+ with various concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.35, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 5, 10, 15 and 20 μM. (b) Plot of the fluorescence intensity against Hg2+ concentration within the range 0–20 μM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. Inset: linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration. (F and F 0 refer to the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs with and without Hg2+, respectively, concentration (λex = 360 nm; λem = 440 nm).

(a) The relative fluorescence intensity (F/F 0) at λex = 340 nm of N, S-CDs aqueous solution in the presence of 5 μM of various metal ions. (b) The relative fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs with/without the presence of 5 μM Hg2+ as the quenching agent, 5 μM Cu2+ as interfering agent and 0.2 mM EDTA as chelating agent to Cu2+: (1) N, S-CDs; (2) N, S-CDs + EDTA; (3) N, S-CDs + Cu2+; (4) N, S-CDs + EDTA + Cu2+; (5) N, S-CDs + Hg2+; (6) N, S-CDs + EDTA + Hg2+. (c) The relative fluorescence intensity of N, S-CD aqueous solution toward 5 μM Hg2+ (red bar), and interference of 5 μM of other metal ions with 5 μM Hg2+ (blue bars). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of 50 μM Hg2+ aqueous solution, N, S-CDs aqueous solution and the N, S-CDs aqueous solution containing 50 μM Hg2+. (b) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs with and without the addition of Hg2+. (c) TEM image of the N, S-CDs with the addition of Hg2+.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of N, S-CDs and the Hg2+ determination based on the N, S-CDs.
Comparison of Hg2+ detection performance based on carbon dot fluorescent sensors.
Determinations of Hg2+ in water and rice samples by the present method.

head.ce:title: Polyethylenimine-functionalized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots as effective fluorescent probes for detection of Hg2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface modification of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N, S-CDs) were performed using cysteine and polyethylenimine as raw materials. The prepared N, S-CDs exhibited excitation-independent in the range of 300–380 nm. Furthermore, mercury(II) ions (Hg2+) can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs. Based on this, we developed a fluorescence sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity to detect Hg2+. Under optimized conditions, the sensor showed good linearity in the range of 0–500 nM, and the limit of detection is 9.2 nM. Further, the sensor showed high sensitivity to Hg2+ in lake water and rice samples. The recovery of the Hg2+ in lake water and rice samples ranged between 98.2 % and 109.5 % with a relative standard deviation below 5.8 %. With outstanding sensitivity and selectivity, the fluorescence sensor provides a promising platform for monitoring Hg2+ in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the harm of heavy metal ions to human health and environmental pollution has become a major global problem. Among many toxic heavy metals, mercury is highly poisonous and can exist in the environment for a long time. It is a persistent pollutant that is not readily biodegradable. Mercury can accumulate in the human body through the food chain. Even if the concentration is low, it will cause damage to the digestive system, respiratory system, circulatory system and nervous system [1,2]. Therefore, developing a simple and environmentally friendly method to detect trace mercury is urgent. So far, many scholars have established many methods for the detection of mercury ions, including atomic absorption spectroscopy [3,4], ICP–MS [5], X-ray absorption spectroscopy [6] and polarography [7]. These methods can provide better accuracy and sensitivity, but the commonly used instruments are expensive and require professional personnel to operate. In recent years, various fluorescence sensors, including organic molecules, luminescence metal organic framework nanosheets [8], semiconductor quantum dots, metal nanoparticles [9] and metal nanoclusters [10] have shown great potential in the sensitive detection of metal ions due to their high sensitivity, fast analysis speed and facile steps and procedures. However, these fluorescent probes also have problems such as expensive synthesis routes, high toxicity, poor photostability or low biocompatibility, which limit their practical applications [11].

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of luminescent nanomaterial with sizes below 10 nm, were first discovered in 2004 [12]. CDs exhibit strong fluorescence, and their fluorescence emission varies with excitation wavelength, particle size and so on. CDs have the advantages of good water solubility, superior photostability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility in comparison to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [13–15]. These merits contribute to the many exciting application prospects in biological imaging [16], biological sensors [17], photocatalysis [18], photovoltaic devices [19], disease diagnosis and drug delivery [20]. Up to now, in addition to the differences in raw materials, there are also various synthesis methods for the synthesis of carbon dots, such as arc discharge, laser ablation [21], combustion [22], electrochemical exfoliation [23], ultrasonic treatment [24], hydrothermal treatment [25,26], pyrolysis [27], acid oxidation [28] and microwave-assisted methods [29,30], among them, the hydrothermal method is environmentally friendly and cheap to synthesize CDs and has been widely used. Although there are many synthesis methods, the synthesized carbon dots still have the disadvantages of low fluorescence quantum yield, poor optical performance and high detection limit. Therefore, some strategies should be adopted to improve the optical properties of CDs, such as doping and surface functionalization. Heteroatom doping, especially nitrogen doping or co-doping of nitrogen with another element, has attracted the attention of many researchers. For example, Zhang's group prepared N-doped carbon dots using folic acid as the only raw material in designing carbon dots, and the relative quantum yield (QY) of nitrogen-doped carbon dots is 15.7 %. Li’s group using citric acid as carbon source, urea as nitrogen source, and l-cysteine as a sulfur source, Wu’s group applied ammonium citrate and l-cysteine, Fu’s group employed ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid salt and thiourea for preparing nitrogen and sulfur co-doped C-dots [31–34]. l-cysteine contains not only carbon but also nitrogen and sulfur. As heteroatom dopants with appropriate energy levels, nitrogen and sulfur help to form new energy levels, thus enhancing blue emission[14]. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) is a hydrophilic polymer with a polyamine structure. It can be used not only as a surface passivating agent for CDs but also as the main framework for synthesising carbon dots. The water solubility, stability, fluorescence properties and biocompatibility of CDs modified by PEI have been improved, and the amino groups provided by PEI can identify analytes. It can also be used for metal ion detection due to its strong chelating effect with some heavy metal ions[35]. Based on the properties of l-cysteine and polyethyleneimine, doping and functionalization were combined in synthesizing carbon points to improve carbon points' optical properties.

In this work, using cysteine (Cys) and polyethylenimine (PEI) as precursors, new N, S-CDs were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method, which integrated synthesis and functionalization. Their surface morphology and functional groups were studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), FT-IR, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). We had studied the quenching effect of mercury ions on the fluorescence of N, S-CDs and the quenching mechanism. The detection method of Hg2+ was established, and the sensitivity and specification were evaluated, which will realize in the application of NCDs-SH in Hg2+ detection.

PEI and Cys were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd (China). Quinine sulfate was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. MgSO4, Hg(NO3)2 H2O, AgNO3, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, MnSO4·5H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, CrCl3·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2 5H2O, FeCl3, FeCl2·4H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, PbSO4, NaCl, KCl, AlCl3, CaCl2 and ZnCl2 were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Plant.

The surface morphology of N, S-CDs were characterized with a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope. FT-IR were measured using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 (Thermo Nicolet, USA) using the potassium bromide wafer technique. A TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Puxi, Beijing, China) was used to measure the absorption spectra of N, S-CDs. The fluorescence spectra of the N, S-CDs were measured with a fluorescence spectrometer RF-6000 (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha + photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was recorded on a SmartLab 9kw, a D8 Advance automatic powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan).

The preparation process of N, S-CDs was as follows: 0.5 g PEI and 0.25 g Cys were added to ultrapure water (30 mL) and dissolved using ultrasound. Next, the solution was heated in an autoclave at 180° C for 5 h. Then, the solution was cooled to room temperature. The reaction solution was filtered with a 0.22 µ M filter membrane and then centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the centrifuged solution was dialyzed (1000 Da) for 24 h to remove small molecules and impurities. Subsequently, the pure solution was kept in the dark for future experiments.

The detection of Hg2+ was carried out in (Phosphate Buffer Solution) PBS at room temperature. First, we studied the sensitivity of Hg2+ to the fluorescence of N, S-CDs. N, S-CDs solution (100 μL, 0.01 mg/mL) and a certain concentration of Hg2+ were added to a centrifuge tube, and then diluted with PBS solution (pH 7.0) to 2.0 mL and mixed thoroughly. The evenly mixed solution was allowed to stand at room temperature for 2 min, and then the fluorescence spectrum was recorded under an excitation of 340 nm. The selectivity of the N, S-CDs fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ was studied by adding other interfering metal ions. The interfering ions included Ag+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+ and Al3+, the concentration of interfering ions was 5 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The N, S-CDs fluorescent sensors application in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, we selected lake water and rice as actual samples to study the effectiveness of N, S-CDs fluorescent sensors for detecting Hg2+ in real samples. Before analysis, the real water and rice samples were pretreated. First, take a certain amount of water sample (Taihu Lake, Wuxi, China), use 0.22 μm membrane filtration to remove impurities, and then centrifuge at 10000 rpm for 15 min. In this work, the rice sample was purchased from a local market in Wuxi. A pressure tank digestion method was used for sample preparation. First, the rice sample was ground into powder, and then the milled rice (1 g) sample and HNO3 (5 mL) were added to the autoclave and heated at 150° C for 5 h. After the reaction, the cooled solution was transferred to a beaker. To remove residual acid, the beaker was heated on an electric hot plate to concentrate its final volume to about 0.5 mL. After cooling to room temperature, a certain amount of ultrapure water was added to the beaker (10 mL), and the diluted solution was used for subsequent experiments.

In the present study, luminescent N, S-CDs were prepared using PEI and Cys as precursors by hydrothermal synthesis strategy, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The shape and microstructure of N, S-CDs were characterized by TEM and HR-TEM. As can be seen from Fig. 1 a, the as-prepared N, S-CDs were uniform in size, approximately circular, and had no apparent aggregation. The average size of N, S-CDs was approximately 2.5 ± 0.1 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). In the HR-TEM diagram of N, S-CDs, we can see that the lattice spacing is 0.34 nm (Fig. 1b), indicating that the composition of N, S-CDs and graphite is similar. XRD analysis of N, S-CDs (Fig. 1c) showed a diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.5°, which further confirmed the results of HR-TEM.

The functional groups and the internal structure of N, S-CDs were studied by FT-IR spectroscopy. In Fig. 1d, the absorption band appearing at 3270 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of OH and NH. The bands at 2936 and 2823 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH. The absorption peaks at 1626 and 1457 cm−1 were designated as the stretching vibrations of the CO bond and CO bond, respectively. The absorption peak at 1371 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of CN. These show that N, S-CDs contain a large amount of –NH2, –OH and –COOH, which made the synthesized N, S-CDs had good hydrophilicity. The peak at 1093 cm−1 belonged to CS bending vibration. There is a weak absorption peak at 655 cm−1, ascribed to the stretching of CS. By analysing the FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CDs, the existence of N-containing and S-containing groups indicated that N and S have been doped into the N, S-CDs skeleton.

The surface charge of the synthesized N, S-CDs was analyzed by a zeta potential analyzer, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The zeta potential value of the N, S-CDs is −10.1 mV, indicating that there are some negatively charged groups on the surface of N, S-CDs, such as the carboxyl group and hydroxyl group. The elemental composition and surface groups of N,S-CDs were analyzed by XPS. The XPS spectrum in Fig. 2 (Fig. 2b) shows three typical peaks at 284.8, 399.2 and 531.0 eV, which belonged to carbon (C), oxygen (O) and nitrogen (N), respectively. In addition, there were two peaks at 163 and 232 eV, which were assigned to S 2p and S 2 s. The spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2c) could be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6 (CC/CC), 285.7 (CS/CN), 287.6 (CO) and 288.4 eV (CO). The spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 2d) showed three peaks at 399.1, 400.2 and 401.3 eV. These peaks corresponded to three types of nitrides: pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2e), the peaks at 530.8 and 534.6 eV belonged to CO and OH groups, respectively. In the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2f), there were two peaks at 163.2 and 164.3ev, which were ascribed to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of thiophene S, respectively [14]. The XPS and FT-IR results confirmed that N and S were successfully doped into the framework of N, S-CDs.

The optical properties of N, S-CDs were studied by UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrometer. In Fig. 3 a, the absorption peak at 334 nm corresponded to the n–π* transition of the CO bond [36], which was caused by the surface state trapping the excited state energy. As shown in Fig. 3a, the optimal excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength of N, S-CDs are 340 nm and 470 nm, respectively. The photo (inset of Fig. 3a) shows the color of the N, S-CDs solution under sunlight and ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) irradiation. The N, S-CDs solution showed strong blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp. As shown in Fig. 3b, when the excitation wavelength was gradually increased from 300 to 380 nm, the maximum emission peak of N, S-CDs was always at 470 nm. Since the emission wavelength of N, S-CDs did not change with the excitation wavelength, N, S-CDs is excitation wavelength-independent. This wavelength-independent fluorescence property of N, S-CDs could be due to the relatively uniform localized surface state and uniform size of N, S-CDs [37,38]. The relative QY of the synthesized N, S-CDs was 16.4 %. The stability of N, S-CDs solution was investigated by irradiating it with ultraviolet light, and the fluorescence intensity hardly changed after 3 h. In addition, even after 6 months of storage at ambient temperature, N, S-CDs did not appear to precipitate nanoparticles, suggesting the good stability of the CDs.

To investigate the tolerance of N, S-CDs to salt ions, we added different amounts of NaCl to the N, S-CDs solution to observe its fluorescence change. It can be seen from Fig. 4 a that when the concentration of NaCl is in the range of 0–1 mol/L, the fluorescence change of N, S-CDs can be negligible. The results indicated that N, S-CDs were highly salt-tolerant and had great potential as fluorescent sensors in physiological applications.

To investigate the effect of acid and alkali on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs, the fluorescence of N, S-CDs was measured at different pH. As shown in Fig. 4b, when the pH value increases from 1 to 4, the luminescence intensity of N, S-CDs increases gradually. When the pH value changed from 4 to 9, the PL intensity of N, S-CDs remained almost unchanged, and then began to decrease when the pH was greater than 9. The variation of fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs at different pH values may be caused by the protonation or deprotonation of its surface groups in acidic or alkaline solutions [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the fluorescence probe for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations (0–20.0 × 10–6 M) were added to the N, S-CDs aqueous solution, and the fluorescence change was recorded to investigate the probe's sensitivity to Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 5 a, Hg2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of the fluorescent probe. With the gradual increase of Hg2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 470 nm gradually decreases. The fluorescence-quenching efficiency showed a good linear correlation over the range from 0 to 5.0 × 10–7 M (Fig. 5b). The linear regression equation was (F 0 – F)/F 0 = 1.32591 [Hg2+] + 0.00113 (R 2 = 0.9975). The limit of detection was estimated to be 9.2 × 10–9 M based on 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of fluorescence without adding Hg2+ (n = 10, ex = 340 nm), and s is the slope of the equation. In addition, we compared the sensing system in this work with previously reported fluorescent probes (Table 1 ). Compared with other fluorescent sensors, the fluorescent probe prepared in this experiment had higher sensitivity in detecting Hg2+. This stable and inexpensive fluorescent probe exhibited high sensitivity to Hg2+ and was suitable for monitoring Hg2+ in environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the fluorescent probe for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In the experiment, to evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescent probe for Hg2+, the effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs probe was studied. The metal ions used in this experiment included Ag+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+ and Al3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 6 a, only Hg2+ can significantly quench the fluorescence of N, S-CDs. In addition to Hg2+, Cu2+ also has some quenching effect, but the quenching efficiency is lower than that of Hg2+. To eliminate the interference of Cu2+ ions, we chose the chelating agent EDTA as the masking agent. As shown in Fig. 6b, the presence of EDTA had no noticeable effect on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs, but EDTA could restore the fluorescence quenched by Cu2+. A comparative experiment was conducted to investigate the influence of Hg coexisting with other ions on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs. In Fig. 6c, the red bar represents 5 μM Hg2+ alone, and the blue bars represent a mixture of 5 μM Hg2+ and 5 μM other metal ions. In Fig. 6c, when other metal ions coexist with Hg2+, the influence of other metal ions on the probe's fluorescence is almost negligible. The results show that the N, S-CDs sensor can selectively detect Hg2+ in the mixture.

We conducted the following experiments to study the mechanism of N, S-CDs as fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+. Fig. 7 a shown the UV–vis spectra of N, S-CDs, N, S-CDs + Hg2+, and Hg2+ (50 μM). The UV–vis spectrum analysis showed that the intensity and position of the absorption band at 340 nm of N, S-CDs hardly changed after Hg2+ addition compared with that without Hg2+, indicating that no complex was formed between Hg2+ and N, S-CDs. Therefore, the mechanism of Hg2+ quenching N, S-CDs was preliminarily inferred to be dynamic quenching. To understand further the PL quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of N, S-CDs before and after quenching by Hg2+ were studied. From Fig. 7b, we can see that in the absence of Hg2+, the average fluorescence lifetime of N, S-CDs was 4.1 ns, indicating a fast exciton recombination process. However, when Hg2+ was added, the fluorescence lifetime of carbon dots decreased to 3.0 ns, which further confirmed that the quenching type was dynamic quenching. As shown in Fig. 7c, the TEM of the N, S-CDs did not show an aggregated state after adding mercury ions, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of the N, S-CDs was not aggregation-induced. By incorporating N and S in the synthesis of N, S-CDs can significantly change their local electron density and affect the distribution of excitons. Hg2+ may form coordination bonds with functional groups (such as amino, hydroxyl or carboxyl groups) on the surface of N, S-CDs and an effective effective photoinduced electron transfer process occurs, which promotes the non-radiative electron/hole composite annihilation, thereby quenching the fluorescence of N, S-CDs [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of N, S-CDs-based Hg2+ sensor in lake water and rice samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of the sensor in actual samples, we used the probe to detect Hg2+ in lake water and rice samples. Hg2+ with various concentrations was added to the pretreated lake water and rice samples, and then the changes of probe fluorescence were recorded. As shown in Table 2 , Hg2+ could not be detected in the two real samples, the recovery of Hg2+ in lake water and rice samples was in the range of 98.2 % to 109.5 %, and the relative standard deviation varied from 2.3 % to 5.8 %. The results show that N, S-CDs can be used as effective fluorescent probes to detect Hg2+ in actual samples with high sensitivity and selectivity.

Doping and functionalization are important means to improve the photoluminescence performance of CDs. Based on this, we prepared nitrogen sulfur Co-doped and amino-functionalized carbon dots by one-step hydrothermal method. In the excitation wavelength range of 300–380 nm, the emission wavelength does not change with the change of the excitation wavelength. In addition, the synthesized N, S-CDs have good light stability, and are resistant to acid and salt. In the selective investigation, only Cu2+ showed interference to the detection of Hg2+, and the use of chelating agent EDTA can effectively mask the interference of Cu2+, and achieve ultra-high specific detection of Hg2+. In addition, we used the sensor for the spiked recovery of Hg2+ in actual water and rice samples, and the results showed good reproducibility and accuracy.

Funding This study was funded by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2020YFC1606800), the National first-class discipline program of Food Science and Technology (JUFSTR20180509), Science and technology project of Jiangsu Bureau of Quality and Technical Supervision (KJ175923 and KJ185646) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (2018 M642165).
Compliance with Ethical Standards
Informed Consent: Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
Data Availability Statements: All data generated or analysed during this study are included in this published article.
Ethical Approval: This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

Kunfeng Liu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation. Chongshu Xia: Visualization, Methodology. Yahui Guo: Software, Validation. Hang Yu: Visualization, Methodology. Yunfei Xie: Software, Validation. Weirong Yao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a rhodamine-rhodanine-based fluorescent mercury sensor and its use to monitor real-time uptake and distribution of inorganic mercury in live zebrafish larvae
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bera, K., A.K. Das, M. Nag, S. Basak, Development of a rhodamine-rhodanine-based fluorescent mercury sensor and its use to monitor real-time uptake and distribution of inorganic mercury in live zebrafish larvae, Anal. Chem. 86 (5) (2014) 2740-2746.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Sens. Actuators, B.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Qin, X., W. Lu, A.M. Asiri, A.O. Al-Youbi, X. Sun, Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions, Sens. Actuators, B. 184 (2013) 156-162.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective determination of free dissolved chlorine using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe
Microchim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lin, Y., B. Yao, T. Huang, S. Zhang, X. Cao, W. Weng, Selective determination of free dissolved chlorine using nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe, Microchim. Acta. 183 (7) (2016) 2221-2227.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of mercury in water and fish samples by cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry after solid phase extraction on agar modified with 2-mercaptobenzimidazole
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Pourreza, N., K. Ghanemi, Determination of mercury in water and fish samples by cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry after solid phase extraction on agar modified with 2-mercaptobenzimidazole, J. Hazard. Mater. 161 (2-3) (2009) 982-987.
